t: Deliberate induction of physical or psychological symptoms or harm in another person (victim).
Patient presents others as the victim, ill, or injured.
Deceptive behavior with no apparent material gain.
The behavior is not better explained by another mental disorder.
Diagnosis is placed on the file, not on the victim.
APSAC, AAP — child protection protocols; this clinical condition also
Clinical suspicion — atypical symptom pattern, related to mother.
Pediatrician, psychiatrist, social worker multidisciplinary team.
Obtaining medical documentation from other clinics.
Separation test — monitoring symptoms when the patient is separated from the caregiver.
Video monitoring (under child protection and legal intervention)
Toxicological and biochemical tests (evidence of creation).
Referral to child protection and legal coordination is MANDatory.
The deliberate induction does not exist; it expresses medical suspicions but does not create them.
Physical abuse, sexual abuse, neglect — different motivation.
Social service and child protection referral.
Victim's safety is the first priority
Medical occupational context - occupational prohibitions may be considered.
Family intervention and sibling protection assessment.
Coordination of the legal system is mandatory.
— Absolutely when suspicion exists; APSAC, AAP protocols.
Pediatrician + psychiatrist + social worker + forensic specialist.
The patient is separated from the primary caregiver; symptoms are observed.
For the victim – high mortality (6–9%) and morbidity without intervention.
For the patient — improvement with psychotherapy; long-term follow-up.
Myth 1: "The mother is truly caring, the diagnosis is wrong"
Clinical features (mother-attributed symptoms, atypical medical history) require substantial proof; clinical suspicion is sufficient for referral to child protection.
Myth 2: "This condition is not a psychiatric problem; it is just child abuse"
both approaches important—child protection + perpetrator psychiatric treatment.
Myth 3: "Treatment of the perpetrator delays cessation of care for the victim"
Victim safety first priority; psychiatric treatment separately continued.
Myth 4: "It only occurs in the mother"
In most cases mother, but other caregivers (fathers, caregivers, healthcare professionals) can also be perpetrator.
Confrontation may necessitate transfer to another clinical unit; the patient is being followed up; child protection and forensic coordination are mandatory.
WHO. ICD-11. 6D51 Factitious disorder imposed on another. 2024.
Meadow R. Munchausen syndrome by proxy. The hinterland of child abuse. Lancet 1977;2(8033):343–345.
Sheridan M.S. The deceit continues: an updated literature review of Munchausen Syndrome by Proxy. Child Abuse Negl 2003;27(4):431–451.
APSAC Task Force. Munchausen by Proxy: Identification, Intervention, and Case Management. 2018.
